spl:
search `notable` urgency=critical earliest=-90d
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen, latest(_time) as last_touch by rule_name, owner
| eval window_sec=last_touch-first_seen
description: Measures how quickly executives or duty managers acknowledge sev-1 security alerts, evidencing GV.OV oversight of outcomes.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST CSF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Define critical urgency mapping; (2) use notable status transitions if available; (3) monthly governance review; (4) document remediation for outliers.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern â€” correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
â€˘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263).
â€˘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `notable` macro (urgency, status, owner, _time).
â€˘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 â€” Configure data collection
(1) Define critical urgency mapping; (2) use notable status transitions if available; (3) monthly governance review; (4) document remediation for outliers.

Step 2 â€” Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
search `notable` urgency=critical earliest=-90d
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen, latest(_time) as last_touch by rule_name, owner
| eval window_sec=last_touch-first_seen
```

Understanding this SPL

**Executive Oversight Dashboard â€” Mean Time to Acknowledge Critical Alerts (GV.OV-01)** â€” Measures how quickly executives or duty managers acknowledge sev-1 security alerts, evidencing GV.OV oversight of outcomes.

Documented **Data sources**: `notable` macro (urgency, status, owner, _time). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedâ€”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

â€˘ Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
â€˘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by rule_name, owner** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
â€˘ `eval` defines or adjusts **window_sec** â€” often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.


Step 3 â€” Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 â€” Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Column chart, Table
grandmaExplanation: We watch for measures how quickly executives or duty managers acknowledge sev-1 security alerts, evidencing gv.ov oversight of outcomes. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
